skill_areas.name: Water Confidence & Safety
skill_areas.description: Overcoming fear of water, learning to float, basic water safety, and building comfort in the pool for absolute beginners
pain_points: Fear of putting face in water
Panic when feet cannot touch the bottom
Cannot float on back or front
Anxiety about deep water
Previous bad experience or trauma with water
common_mistakes: Trying to learn strokes before being comfortable in water
Tensing up the body which prevents floating
Holding breath tightly instead of relaxed exhale
Gripping the wall or lane rope out of fear
Skipping gradual depth progression
drills: Standing water familiarization - splash face, pour water over head
Bobbing drill - stand in shallow water, dip face in, blow bubbles
Starfish float (front) with wall support, then without
Back float with head resting on water, arms out
Mushroom float (tuck knees, hug, float)
Wall push-off and glide to standing depth

skill_areas.name: Freestyle (Front Crawl)
skill_areas.description: Efficient body position, bilateral breathing, high elbow catch and pull, and flutter kick for the fastest and most versatile stroke
pain_points: Head position too high causing legs to sink
Breathing technique causes loss of balance
Crossing over on hand entry (crossing midline)
Inefficient kick using too much energy
Dropped elbow during catch phase
No body rotation - swimming flat
Thumb-first entry causing shoulder impingement
common_mistakes: Looking forward instead of down (raises head, sinks legs)
Wide hand entry outside shoulder line
Straight arm pull instead of high elbow catch
Kicking from the knees instead of the hips
Holding breath instead of continuous exhale underwater
Over-rotating the body during breathing
drills: Catch-up drill for stroke timing
Side kick drill for body rotation
Fingertip drag drill for high elbow recovery
6-3-6 drill for balance and rotation
Fist drill for improving catch feel
Single arm freestyle with other arm extended
Shark fin drill for high elbow recovery
Paddle scull drill for catch awareness

skill_areas.name: Backstroke
skill_areas.description: Supine body position, arm recovery and entry, hip rotation, and steady flutter kick on back with proper head stability
pain_points: Sinking hips and legs
Swimming crooked (no lane awareness)
Poor arm entry angle (too wide or crossing over)
Insufficient body rotation
Water going up the nose
Fear of not seeing where you are going
common_mistakes: Sitting up in the water instead of lying flat
Head too far forward (chin to chest) instead of neutral
Bending arms too early on the pull
Kicking with bent knees (bicycle kick)
Not using backstroke flags for wall awareness
Stopping rotation which flattens the stroke
drills: Single arm backstroke drill
6-kick switch drill for rotation
Backstroke with cup on forehead (head stability)
Double arm backstroke for pull pattern
Spin drill for rotation awareness
Backstroke kick with arms at sides for body position

skill_areas.name: Breaststroke
skill_areas.description: Timing of pull, breathe, kick, glide sequence and developing a powerful whip kick with proper streamline recovery
pain_points: Scissor kick instead of whip kick
Poor timing between pull and kick
Head lifting too high causing drag
Knees dropping too wide and low creating drag
Not gliding long enough in streamline
Arms pulling too wide past shoulders
common_mistakes: Pulling arms past hip line (too far back)
Breathing by lifting head up instead of pressing chest forward
Asymmetric kick (one leg stronger than other)
Rush timing - pulling and kicking simultaneously instead of sequentially
Not finishing the kick with feet together pointed
Looking forward instead of down during glide phase
drills: 2-kick 1-pull drill for kick emphasis
Breaststroke kick on back (watch feet for symmetry)
Streamline glide drill for timing
Wall kick drill with hands on deck watching foot path
Pull with dolphin kick to isolate pull pattern
Breaststroke pull with flutter kick for pull timing
3-second glide drill for streamline patience

skill_areas.name: Butterfly
skill_areas.description: Undulating body dolphin kick, simultaneous arm recovery over water, breathing rhythm, and building butterfly-specific endurance
pain_points: Cannot maintain body undulation
Arms not clearing the water on recovery
Breathing timing disrupts rhythm
Exhaustion after one length (25m)
Two kicks not synced with arm cycle
Hips not rising during recovery phase
common_mistakes: Kicking only from the knees instead of full body wave
Breathing forward/up instead of low and forward
Single kick per stroke instead of two kicks
Over-pulling past hips which delays recovery
Entering hands too wide (should be shoulder width)
Trying to muscle through instead of using rhythm
drills: Underwater dolphin kick drill (on front and back)
Single arm butterfly drill (one arm at side)
3-3-3 drill (3 right, 3 left, 3 full)
Butterfly kick on back with arms overhead
2-kick-1-pull drill for timing
Butterfly with fins for rhythm development
Vertical dolphin kick in deep end for core strength

skill_areas.name: Flip Turns & Open Turns
skill_areas.description: Flip turns for freestyle and backstroke, open turns for breaststroke and butterfly, approach timing, and wall push-offs
pain_points: Losing speed approaching the wall
Not enough push-off force from the wall
Poor streamline during underwater phase
Misjudging distance from wall
Getting water up the nose during flip
Twisting to wrong direction on backstroke flip turn
common_mistakes: Gliding into the wall instead of swimming into the turn
Taking an extra stroke or half-stroke before flipping
Not blowing air out through nose during the flip
Pushing off on back instead of rotating to side/front
Shallow push-off without proper streamline
Breaking streamline too early after push-off
Not counting strokes from backstroke flags
drills: Somersault practice in open water (mid-pool)
Approach and flip turn with decreasing distance
Streamline push-off and breakout drill
No-breath-into-the-wall drill for approach speed
Backstroke flag counting drill (strokes from flags to wall)
Open turn touch-and-go speed drill
Underwater streamline kick for 5-8 meters off every wall

skill_areas.name: Dive Starts & Block Starts
skill_areas.description: Racing dive from blocks, track start vs grab start, entry angle, streamline breakout, and relay exchange timing
pain_points: Belly-flopping on entry instead of clean entry
Fear of diving from blocks
Entering too deep and losing momentum
Entering too shallow and not getting depth for breakout
Slow reaction to start signal
Poor balance on the block before the start
common_mistakes: Looking up at the signal instead of looking down at the water
Jumping up instead of driving forward and out
Arms not in streamline before hitting the water
Not using back foot (track start) for power
Flat entry angle instead of hole-in-the-water entry
Coming up too early from the start
drills: Sitting dive from pool edge (beginner progression)
Kneeling dive from pool edge
Standing dive from pool edge
Block start with focus on forward drive
Reaction time practice with verbal commands
Entry angle practice - dive through a hoop target
Start-to-breakout practice (15m sprint)

skill_areas.name: Breathing Technique
skill_areas.description: Rhythmic breathing, bilateral breathing, continuous exhale underwater, breath control, and managing breathing anxiety
pain_points: Holding breath instead of exhaling underwater
Lifting head to breathe instead of rotating
Panic and anxiety when face is in water
Cannot breathe on both sides (bilateral)
Running out of breath after one length
Swallowing water when trying to breathe
common_mistakes: Explosive exhale at the surface instead of steady underwater exhale
Turning head too far to breathe (looking at ceiling)
Inhaling through the nose instead of mouth
Not exhaling fully so lungs are not empty for quick inhale
Breathing every stroke instead of every 2-3 strokes
Tensing neck and jaw during breathing
drills: Bobbing drill (exhale underwater, inhale above)
Bilateral breathing sets (breathe every 3 strokes)
Breath control sets (25m on fewer breaths progressively)
Standing face-in-water exhale practice
Crocodile eyes drill (eyes at water level, mouth submerged, exhale)
Trickle breathing - slow continuous exhale through nose and mouth
Hypoxic sets (breathe every 5, 7 strokes for advanced)

skill_areas.name: Endurance & Pacing
skill_areas.description: Building aerobic swim base, interval training, CSS (Critical Swim Speed), negative splitting, and race pacing strategy
pain_points: Going out too fast and dying at the end
Cannot swim continuously for long distances
No sense of pace per 100m
Getting bored during long sets
Technique falling apart when tired
common_mistakes: Only swimming at one speed (no variation in intensity)
Skipping rest intervals and swimming junk yardage
Not tracking times or distances for progress
Ignoring stroke count (SPL) as a metric
Not tapering before competitions
Too much high-intensity, not enough aerobic base
drills: Descending sets (each repeat faster than the last)
CSS (Critical Swim Speed) test: time 400m and 200m, calculate CSS pace
Negative split practice (second half faster than first)
Broken swims (race distance with 10-second rests to learn pacing)
Stroke count per length (SPL) monitoring during endurance sets
Progressive 100s: build speed across a set of 8-10 x 100m
Test set: 10 x 100m on fixed interval, track consistency

skill_areas.name: Open Water Swimming
skill_areas.description: Sighting, drafting, navigation, wave and current management, wetsuit swimming, and open water race tactics
pain_points: Cannot swim straight without lane lines
Panic in deep water without a visible bottom
Sighting disrupts stroke rhythm
Dealing with waves, chop, and currents
Getting kicked or bumped by other swimmers in mass starts
Cold water shock
common_mistakes: Sighting too frequently which slows pace
Lifting head too high to sight (hips drop)
Not practicing in open water before race day
Wearing a brand-new wetsuit on race day
Swimming alone in open water (safety risk)
Ignoring water conditions and weather
drills: Sighting drill: lift eyes to waterline every 6-8 strokes
Crocodile eyes sighting (eyes barely break surface)
Tarzan drill (head-up freestyle for 25m)
Draft swimming practice (swim behind and beside a partner)
Beach entry and exit practice (running in shallow water)
Navigation practice: swim to a fixed point without lane lines
Wetsuit practice sessions before race day

skill_areas.name: Underwater Swimming & Dolphin Kick
skill_areas.description: Streamline body position, underwater dolphin kick off walls and starts, breakout timing, and maximizing underwater distance
pain_points: Cannot hold streamline properly underwater
Dolphin kick too slow or too small
Breaking out too early (wasting free speed)
Breaking out too late (losing speed and oxygen)
Body not fully tight in streamline
common_mistakes: Hands not stacked properly in streamline (gap between hands)
Head poking up out of streamline
Kicking only from knees instead of core-driven wave
Not exhaling slightly through nose (water goes up nose)
Same kick frequency regardless of distance
Breaking streamline with arms before fully surfaced
drills: Streamline push-off: count kicks to 10m, then 12m, then 15m
Underwater dolphin kick on back for visual feedback
Vertical dolphin kick in deep end (treading position)
Streamline squeeze drill on deck (partner checks tightness)
Breakout timing practice: push off, kick 5-7 kicks, breakout at speed
Hypoxic streamline: push off, glide as far as possible on one breath

skill_areas.name: Kids & Toddler Swimming
skill_areas.description: Age-appropriate water skills for children 3-12, water safety education, fun learning through games, and progression to basic strokes
pain_points: Child is terrified of water or pool
Child won't put face in water
Short attention span during lessons
Parent anxiety about child's safety
Child can paddle but not proper strokes
Transitioning from floaties to independent swimming
common_mistakes: Forcing a scared child into the water
Using arm floaties too long (creates bad body position habits)
Not enrolling in structured lessons with qualified instructor
Skipping water safety education
Comparing child's progress to other children
Teaching strokes before water confidence is established
drills: Blow bubbles game (exhale through mouth making bubbles)
Ring/toy retrieval from shallow water
Assisted back float with parent support
Kick on a kickboard across shallow end
Jump into pool (to parent or coach)
Streamline glide with push from wall
Red light green light game (start/stop swimming)

skill_areas.name: Pool Etiquette & Lane Swimming
skill_areas.description: Rules and customs of shared pool swimming: lane speeds, circle swimming, passing, resting at the wall, and general pool courtesy
pain_points: Not knowing which lane speed to join
Confusion about circle swimming direction
Getting stuck behind slower swimmers
Anxiety about being judged for being slow
Not knowing when/where to rest
common_mistakes: Stopping in the middle of the lane to rest
Swimming breaststroke in the fast lane
Not tapping feet to signal passing
Pushing off just as someone is approaching the wall
Wearing jewelry or loose clothing in the pool
Not showering before entering the pool
drills: Practice circle swimming pattern in an empty lane
Learn the tap-and-pass protocol
Practice wall stops (corner, not center)
Read and follow your pool's posted lane rules

skill_areas.name: Swim Workout Planning
skill_areas.description: Structuring swim sessions with warm-up, main set, drills, kick sets, pull sets, and cool-down. Periodization and weekly planning.
pain_points: No idea how to structure a pool session
Just swimming back and forth without purpose
Not knowing what distance or pace to target
Boredom from lack of variety
Not knowing how many sessions per week
common_mistakes: Skipping warm-up and diving straight into fast swimming
Every session is the same (no periodization)
Not including drill work in sessions
Only swimming freestyle and ignoring other strokes
No cool-down after hard sets
Too much volume too soon (injury risk)
drills: Sample beginner session: 200 warm-up, 4x50 drill, 6x50 main set, 200 cool-down (800m total)
Sample intermediate session: 400 warm-up, 4x100 pull, 8x50 kick, 6x100 main set, 200 cool-down (2200m total)
Sample advanced session: 800 warm-up, 8x100 pull, 10x50 kick, 5x200 main set, 6x50 sprint, 400 cool-down (4000m total)
Weekly plan: 3-4 sessions for fitness, 5-6 for competitive, 2-3 for triathlon (combined with bike/run)

skill_areas.name: Dryland Training for Swimmers
skill_areas.description: Out-of-pool exercises to build swim-specific strength, core stability, shoulder health, flexibility, and injury prevention
pain_points: Weak core causing poor body position in water
Shoulder pain from swimming without strength work
No access to gym or dryland equipment
Not knowing which exercises transfer to swimming
Tight ankles limiting kick effectiveness
common_mistakes: Heavy weight training that builds bulk but not swim-speed
Ignoring shoulder prehab (rotator cuff work)
Not stretching ankles for better kick range
Only doing upper body and ignoring core and legs
Doing dryland on the same day as hard pool sessions
Skipping warm-up before dryland exercises
drills: Resistance band pull-apart for shoulder health (3x15)
Resistance band swim pull simulation (3x12 each arm)
Plank hold: front 60s, side 45s each side
Superman hold for back extension (3x30s)
Medicine ball slam for explosive power (3x10)
Ankle stretches: sit on feet (seiza position) for kick flexibility
Squat jumps for start and turn power (3x10)
Lat pulldown or resistance band lat pull for pull strength

recreational.description: Swimming for fun, relaxation, and basic fitness. May swim 1-2 times per week. Wants to enjoy the water without competitive pressure.

fitness_swimmer.description: Swimming primarily for cardio, weight management, and overall fitness. Swims 3-4 times per week. Wants structured workouts and steady improvement.

competitive_swimmer.description: Training for swim meets, school/college/club competitions, or national-level events. Focused on all four strokes, turns, starts, and race tactics.

triathlete.description: Swimming as part of triathlon training. Needs efficient freestyle, open water skills, and endurance without burning out for bike and run legs.

kids_beginners.description: Children aged 3-12 learning to swim. Focus on water safety, confidence, and progressive skill development through fun and games.
